ending: Every principal conspirator is dead. Reacher kills Mayor Teale, Kliner, and the mole Sergeant Baker in the warehouse assault, and, with Finlay, finishes the FBI traitor Picard; Kliner's son had already drowned days earlier, and the earlier dead include Chief Morrison and his wife, the detective Gray, the Treasury source Molly Beth Gordon, a Princeton scholar, and several hired killers. Kliner is confirmed as the man who personally shot Joe Reacher. The warehouse, its forty-ton stockpile of counterfeit cash, and the Margrave police station all burn, and the Kliner Foundation's hold on the town collapses. Roscoe, Charlie Hubble, and the Hubble children are rescued unharmed; Paul Hubble returns to his family to face an uncertain future amid the investigation. State police, the FBI, the National Guard, the Treasury, and the state governor descend on Margrave, whose police force is reduced to four, including Finlay and Roscoe, who both stay to rebuild the town; Roscoe considers running for mayor. Reacher, unwilling to spend years detained by the multi-agency inquiry, chooses to leave, and he and Roscoe part by mutual, amicable decision. He arranges Joe's funeral and boards a bus for California with only Joe's photograph and Roscoe's memorized phone number. Several threads stay open: the identity of the third man who helped stage Gray's murder is never confirmed, Kliner's frightened wife and her fate are never explained, and Reacher's own future, back on the road alone, is entirely unwritten.
in_short: Drifter Jack Reacher is arrested for a murder in the small Georgia town of Margrave, only to discover the dead man is his own brother, Joe, a Treasury investigator. Joe had been closing in on a vast counterfeiting operation run by the town's revered benefactor, Kliner: genuine one-dollar bills are bought nationwide, bleached at a Venezuela plant into authentic currency paper, and reprinted as flawless hundreds, with the Kliner Foundation buying the whole town's silence. As Reacher investigates with the detective Finlay and the officer Roscoe, who becomes his lover, the conspiracy kills to protect itself, reaching into the police, the mayor's office, and even the FBI, whose agent Picard proves to be the final hidden conspirator. After a week of deductions and killings, Reacher storms the warehouse and destroys the operation, killing Kliner, Mayor Teale, the mole Baker, and Picard; the stockpiled cash and the town's corruption go up in flames. Roscoe stays to rebuild Margrave; Reacher, unwilling to be detained, parts from her and leaves for California, alone again.

recaps.text: Jack Reacher, a drifter passing through, is arrested for murder less than an hour after walking into the small, unnaturally tidy Georgia town of Margrave. A body has been found at a warehouse owned by a local businessman named Kliner, and a witness described a man matching Reacher. Reacher is certain his account will clear him: he arrived on foot that morning and killed no one. The hostile police chief, Morrison, wants him held and even claims to have seen him at the scene, but the careful chief of detectives, Finlay, doubts the case. The dead man was shot with a silenced weapon, savagely beaten after death, and carried a hidden phone number belonging to a local banker, Paul Hubble. Brought in, Hubble panics and inexplicably confesses to the killing, though he has an alibi. Reacher and Hubble are shipped to a state prison for the weekend, where they are mistakenly placed among dangerous inmates and attacked; Reacher fights them off. Shaken, Hubble finally admits his confession was false and that he and his family have been threatened with torture and death if he ever talks about something very big.

Reacher has been arrested for a murder at a Margrave warehouse and jailed for the weekend alongside Paul Hubble, the local banker whose hidden phone number was found on the victim. In prison, someone tries to have Reacher killed: he and Hubble are deliberately placed among violent convicts and a gang stages an assassination attempt, which Reacher survives before a warden abruptly moves them to safety. The attempt is later understood to have been arranged from outside. Hubble opens up: the dead man was a private investigator he secretly hired to help him escape and expose a large criminal operation he is trapped in, one involving roughly ten people and building toward some deadline the coming Sunday. He refuses to say more, terrified for his family. Once Reacher's alibi is fully verified, both men are released, but Hubble then vanishes from his own home, leaving everything behind. Meanwhile a second body turns up near the warehouse, killed the same night with the same silenced weapon and identified only by a watch reading 'to Sherman.' Then a fingerprint match on the first victim, keyed to distinctive childhood medical history, reveals the man's identity to Reacher: the murdered stranger at the warehouse is his own brother. What began as a wrongful arrest has become personal.

The murder that got Reacher arrested in Margrave turns out to be family: the dead man at the warehouse is his older brother, Joe, a Treasury Department investigator. A second victim, killed the same night, is identified as Sherman Stoller. Reacher, who has begun a relationship with the sympathetic officer Roscoe, decides to stay and settle Joe's death himself rather than leave it to the police. The reluctant informant Paul Hubble, who admitted his family was threatened over a huge criminal operation and that Joe was the investigator he hired, disappears from his home, and Reacher fears he has been killed. Then the town's hostile chief, Morrison, and his wife are discovered tortured and murdered in their home, the violence matching the threats made against Hubble. Reacher realizes the killings are all connected: Margrave has had no homicides in thirty years and suddenly has several in days. He works out that Morrison was part of the operation, punished for a failure at the warehouse and killed as a warning to whoever replaces him, meaning the next chief will be compromised too. Reacher decides he can trust only the detective Finlay and Roscoe. The wealthy Kliner family, whose foundation appears to sustain the whole town, sits at the center of his suspicions, and Kliner's menacing son has begun openly confronting Reacher over Roscoe.

Reacher, avenging his murdered brother Joe, has narrowed his trust to two allies: the detective Finlay and the officer Roscoe, now his lover. After Chief Morrison and his wife are found tortured and killed, Margrave's elderly mayor, Grover Teale, seizes personal control of the police and openly steers the investigation away from the truth. Finlay quietly brings in an old friend, the FBI agent Picard, who moves Hubble's wife and children to a secret Bureau safe house. Roscoe's own home is then broken into by intruders using the same methods as the Morrison killers; she and Reacher are now targets. Reacher meets the town's benefactor, Kliner, in person and confirms the Kliner Foundation is a financial front, quietly paying local merchants far more than it admits. Roscoe secretly arms Reacher with a dead detective's powerful handgun. When two hitmen tail him, Reacher ambushes and kills them, discovering the missing prison warden's body in their car. Through a Treasury contact of Joe's, Molly Beth Gordon, Reacher learns Joe led an anti-counterfeiting operation and that vast sums of American cash circulate abroad, dependent on trust in the dollar. Finally, tracking the second victim, Sherman Stoller, Reacher and Roscoe reach Stoller's girlfriend Judy, who produces a photograph of Stoller with his old boss, a man they both recognize as Paul Hubble, placing the vanished banker at the heart of the smuggling operation.

Reacher's hunt for his brother Joe's killers now centers on Kliner's counterfeiting operation, run through the town's warehouses and its foundation. Roscoe recognizes Kliner's warehouse in the background of the photograph linking the dead trucker Sherman Stoller to Paul Hubble. From Joe's discarded belongings, Reacher recovers a hidden note headed 'Operation E Unum Pluribus' with a short list of contacts. One of them, the Treasury source Molly Beth Gordon, obtains Joe's secret files and flies to hand them over, but she is stabbed to death at the Atlanta airport and the files are stolen. Following Joe's list, Reacher, Finlay, and Roscoe recover evidence the late detective Gray had hidden in a barbershop: a financial audit exposing the Kliner Foundation as a front and a surveillance log of the warehouse traffic. They also conclude Gray did not kill himself; he was murdered and staged as a suicide, most likely by Morrison, Teale, and a third, unknown man. Reacher mounts a solo surveillance of the warehouses and identifies the guarded compound as the operation's hub. He tails one of its trucks hundreds of miles into Mississippi, where the driver proves to be Kliner's son. Breaking into the parked truck, he expects to find it loaded with money bound for a city, and finds it completely empty, overturning the group's theory of how the operation moves its goods. Everyone connected to Joe's investigation is being hunted, and the Sunday deadline that governs the scheme is only days away.

Reacher and his allies grasp the real shape of Kliner's scheme. The warehouse trucks bring something into Margrave from around the country, not out of it, hidden in air-conditioner boxes marked with strange serial numbers. A Princeton scholar on Joe's contact list is murdered before he can share a breakthrough, and Reacher deduces that the Margrave sergeant Baker is the conspiracy's inside mole, secretly feeding information to Teale. In New York, another expert, Professor Kelstein, confirms that Kliner personally runs a Venezuelan counterfeiting operation producing billions in near-perfect fake hundred-dollar bills, the finest anyone in the trade has seen. The one mystery no one has solved is how Kliner obtains flawless currency paper; the Princeton scholar had found the answer but was killed for it. Back in Margrave, Reacher feeds the mole Baker false information as bait and sets a trap at Hubble's empty house. That night a five-man hit squad in protective suits, led by Kliner's son, arrives to search the house. Reacher kills all five in the dark and the rain, drowning Kliner's son in the swimming pool, and, examining the bodies, recognizes two as warehouse guards and two as police officers, confirming that the corruption runs deeper into the force than Baker, Morrison, and Teale. He disposes of every trace. The Sunday deadline is now under a day away, and Reacher remains troubled by boxes of numbers that hold no actual air conditioners, and by a note of Joe's pointing to the Stollers' garage that he means to reexamine.

Reacher cracks the counterfeiting scheme completely: the operation buys up genuine one-dollar bills across the country, ships them to Margrave, and has them chemically bleached at Kliner's Venezuela plant to yield authentic currency paper, reprinted as hundreds, exactly what Joe's note meant by 'out of one comes many.' He and Finlay confirm it by finding boxes of Stoller's skimmed one-dollar bills in his parents' garage. They brief Finlay's FBI friend Picard, and the trap springs: Picard is himself the operation's tenth and final hidden member, a longtime protector of Kliner inside the Bureau. At gunpoint with Teale and Kliner, Reacher learns that Kliner personally shot Joe, that the Morrisons were tortured to death by Kliner and his son, and that Paul Hubble is alive after all, in hiding. Roscoe, secretly abducted by Picard days earlier, and Finlay are held hostage: unless Hubble is found and Picard reports in, Finlay dies an hour before dawn and Roscoe at dawn. Forced to search for Hubble with Picard guarding him, Reacher works the odds. On the highway near Augusta he provokes a crash, kills the two remaining enemy operatives tailing them, and, after a standoff in which each man swears not to shoot the other, shoots Picard and drives on, leaving his fate uncertain. The dawn deadline is still running, Joe's murder is finally explained, and Reacher is racing alone to find the one man who can lead him back to the hostages before Kliner kills them.

Reacher finds Paul Hubble hiding in Augusta under a false name, and with his help breaks Finlay out of the Margrave jail. Sheltered overnight by the two elderly barbers, who reveal they have hidden fugitives for decades and refused Kliner's money, Reacher also learns the answer to the old mystery that first drew him to town: the blues musician Blind Blake was beaten to death sixty years earlier by the father of a young Grover Teale. Before dawn on Sunday, Reacher, Finlay, and Hubble storm the warehouse. They kill the guard Baker, carry Hubble's sleeping children to safety, and find Roscoe and Charlie Hubble forced to box a fifty-foot mountain of counterfeit cash while Teale and Kliner watch over them with shotguns. In the battle that follows, Reacher shoots Teale and Kliner dead, Kliner confirmed as Joe's killer, and, with Finlay, finally kills the wounded Picard. The warehouse, its forty-ton stockpile, and the police station all burn. Roscoe, Charlie, and the children are rescued unharmed; every conspirator is dead. In the aftermath, state police, the FBI, the National Guard, and the Treasury flood Margrave, and its police force is reduced to four. Roscoe chooses to stay and help rebuild the town, perhaps entering local politics; Reacher, unwilling to be tied up for years in the investigation, decides to leave. The two part by mutual, affectionate agreement. He asks that Joe's ashes be scattered in the meadow where the barber's sister once sang with Blind Blake, then boards a bus for California, resuming his drifting life, carrying only a photograph of Joe and Roscoe's phone number.
